questions.q: Romeo says: 'O brawling love, O loving hate, / O anything of nothing first create!' Who is he talking about, and what does this reveal about his character at the start of the play?
questions.a: He is talking about Rosaline, the woman he is infatuated with before he meets Juliet. The oxymorons ('brawling love', 'loving hate', 'anything of nothing') reveal Romeo as a young man obsessed with the idea of love and prone to overblown, contradictory emotions — he is in love with the idea of being in love, rather than truly understanding it.
questions.hint: It's not about Juliet — this is from Act 1, Scene 1.
questions.explanation: Shakespeare deliberately introduces Romeo as a moody, melodramatic lover before Juliet appears. The contrast between his exaggerated suffering over Rosaline and his quick switch to Juliet is part of how Shakespeare characterises young love as impulsive and changeable. The oxymorons foreshadow the play's central tension: love and hate, life and death.

questions.q: Romeo says: 'But soft! What light through yonder window breaks? / It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.' What is happening at this moment, and what does the metaphor suggest?
questions.a: This is the balcony scene (Act 2, Scene 2). Romeo has secretly climbed into the Capulet orchard and sees Juliet at her window. The metaphor compares Juliet to the sun — implying she is the source of all light and life for him, dazzlingly beautiful, and central to his world. The image also suggests warmth, hope, and a new dawn after the 'darkness' of his earlier obsession with Rosaline.
questions.hint: Romeo is in the orchard at night; Juliet appears at her window.
questions.explanation: The sun metaphor is one of Shakespeare's most famous images. It also has tragic undertones — the sun rises and sets; the brightest light casts the darkest shadows. Romeo's praise of Juliet is hyperbolic but sincere; this scene shows his sudden, complete transformation from melancholy to ecstasy.

questions.q: Romeo says: 'O, I am fortune's fool!' What has just happened, and what does this line mean?
questions.a: He has just killed Tybalt in revenge for Tybalt killing Mercutio (Act 3, Scene 1). The line means he feels he is being mocked or controlled by Fortune — by fate. He realises that despite trying to keep the peace (refusing to fight Tybalt earlier) he has been pushed into doing the very thing that will destroy his future with Juliet.
questions.hint: He has just killed someone — and the consequences are enormous.
questions.explanation: 'Fortune' was personified in Elizabethan thinking as a fickle, powerful goddess. Romeo's cry shows him recognising — too late — that he is caught in forces beyond his control. The line is also a turning point: from this moment, the play moves from comedy-like romance towards inevitable tragedy. The reference to fate echoes the prologue's 'star-crossed lovers'.

questions.q: Romeo says: 'Then I defy you, stars!' What has happened, and what does this defiance show about him?
questions.a: He has just heard the false news that Juliet is dead (Act 5, Scene 1). His defiance of the stars rejects the fate that the prologue warned of — he refuses to accept the destiny mapped out for him, and resolves to take his own life so that he can be with Juliet again. It shows him as bold and decisive but also reckless and impulsive — he doesn't check the news or wait for confirmation.
questions.hint: He has heard tragic news about Juliet and is deciding how to act.
questions.explanation: Romeo's defiance is futile — by trying to escape his fate, he triggers it. He sets out to die in the tomb, where his suicide will cause Juliet's. This is dramatic irony at its sharpest: the audience knows Juliet is alive, but Romeo doesn't. The 'star-crossed lovers' line from the prologue is fulfilled exactly as he tries to defy it.

questions.q: Romeo's last words: 'Here's to my love! [Drinks.] O true apothecary! / Thy drugs are quick. Thus with a kiss I die.' What does this final speech reveal about him?
questions.a: He drinks the poison in the Capulet tomb (Act 5, Scene 3), believing Juliet is dead. The toast 'Here's to my love!' shows his death as an act of love — even in his final moments his focus is entirely on Juliet. The bitter compliment to the apothecary ('quick' meaning fast-acting) is darkly practical. The final 'kiss' shows that love and death are now inseparable for him, completing the play's central theme.
questions.hint: These are his very last lines.
questions.explanation: The 'kiss' echoes their first meeting at the Capulet ball ('You kiss by th' book'). Shakespeare frames Romeo's death not as despair but as a final act of devotion. The tragedy is heightened because the audience knows Juliet will wake moments later. Romeo dies impulsively, decisively, and entirely focused on his love — characterising him fully right to the end.

questions.q: Juliet says: 'My only love sprung from my only hate! / Too early seen unknown, and known too late!' What has just happened, and what does this reveal about her situation?
questions.a: She has just discovered, after kissing Romeo at the ball, that he is a Montague — the family her family is feuding with (Act 1, Scene 5). The antithesis between 'love' and 'hate', 'early' and 'late', captures the trap she is in: she has fallen in love with someone she is supposed to hate. It marks the moment her fate is sealed — she has 'seen' Romeo before knowing who he is, and 'known' him only when it's too late to undo her feelings.
questions.hint: She's just learned his surname at the end of the Capulet ball.
questions.explanation: Juliet's awareness here is striking. Unlike Romeo (whose love language is often hyperbolic and self-focused), Juliet immediately recognises the danger of what has happened. The rhyming couplets and balanced antithesis show her thinking carefully even in the moment of crisis. It's our first sign that she may be more emotionally intelligent than Romeo.

questions.q: Juliet says: 'O Romeo, Romeo! Wherefore art thou Romeo? / Deny thy father and refuse thy name.' What is she actually asking, and what does this reveal about her values?
questions.a: This is the balcony scene (Act 2, Scene 2). 'Wherefore' means 'why', NOT 'where' — she's asking why he has to be a Montague (Romeo). She is asking him to deny his family name so that they can be together. This reveals that she values love above family loyalty and is prepared to challenge the rigid social structures that determine her identity. It shows extraordinary courage and independence of mind for a 13-year-old in a patriarchal society.
questions.explanation: The common misreading of 'wherefore' is a huge giveaway in exams — Juliet isn't looking for Romeo, who is standing right below her. She is questioning the meaning of names: 'What's in a name? That which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet.' She is interrogating the social systems that say her love is wrong, and prepared to defy them.

questions.q: Juliet says: 'My bounty is as boundless as the sea, / My love as deep; the more I give to thee, / The more I have, for both are infinite.' What does this metaphor reveal about her love for Romeo?
questions.a: She compares her love to the sea — vast, boundless, infinite (Act 2, Scene 2, balcony scene). The paradox 'the more I give to thee, the more I have' captures the idea that love is not depleted by being shared; instead it grows. This reveals her love as deep, generous, and mature — not just an infatuation but a transformative force that defines her existence.
questions.hint: The sea is the central image.
questions.explanation: Juliet's love language consistently uses imagery of infinity and depth. Compare with Romeo's love language, which is often more visual (light, the sun, brightness). Where Romeo's love sees Juliet, Juliet's love feels Romeo. Many critics argue Juliet is the more sophisticated and devoted of the two lovers, with this speech often cited as evidence.

questions.q: Juliet says: 'Gallop apace, you fiery-footed steeds, / Towards Phoebus' lodging.' What is happening at this moment, and what does the language reveal?
questions.a: Juliet is impatiently waiting for night to fall, when Romeo will come to her on their wedding night (Act 3, Scene 2). 'Phoebus' is the sun-god; she is commanding the horses pulling the sun's chariot to gallop quickly across the sky so that day will end. The classical reference and the urgent, commanding tone reveal her as passionately desiring Romeo, and also as far more confident and articulate than the typical Elizabethan ideal of a passive young bride.
questions.hint: Day-time imagery for someone waiting impatiently for night.
questions.explanation: This soliloquy is unusual because it is openly sensual — a 13-year-old girl explicitly anticipating her wedding night. Shakespeare gives Juliet remarkable agency and desire. The classical reference also shows her education and intelligence. The audience knows that Romeo has just been banished for killing Tybalt — Juliet doesn't know yet, which makes this speech painfully ironic.

questions.q: Juliet's last words: 'O happy dagger! / This is thy sheath; there rust, and let me die.' What is happening, and what do these final words tell us about her?
questions.a: She is in the Capulet tomb (Act 5, Scene 3), having just woken to find Romeo dead from poison. Hearing the watchmen approaching, she stabs herself with Romeo's dagger. The startling word 'happy' (meaning fortunate, opportune) shows her welcoming death as a reunion with Romeo. The image of her body as a 'sheath' for the dagger is intimate and shocking — she frames her suicide as a kind of consummation, an act of love. Her decisiveness contrasts with earlier moments and shows her growth from a sheltered daughter to someone making her own ultimate choice.
questions.hint: These are her very last words. She has Romeo's dagger.
questions.explanation: Juliet's death is, on the page, more violent than Romeo's — he drinks poison, she stabs herself. The sexual undertone ('sheath') is deliberate: Shakespeare links love and death throughout the play, and Juliet's final act unites the two. Her last words show her as fully in control of her own ending, completing her transformation across the play from compliant daughter to autonomous tragic heroine.

questions.q: What is an oxymoron, and why does Shakespeare use so many of them in Romeo and Juliet?
questions.a: An oxymoron is a figure of speech where two contradictory words are placed together (e.g., 'loving hate', 'happy dagger', 'cold fire'). Shakespeare uses them throughout the play to capture the contradictions at the heart of his characters' experience: love mixed with hate, joy mixed with sorrow, life mixed with death. They reflect the play's central tension and the confused, paradoxical state of being in love.
questions.hint: Two opposite words placed together for effect.
questions.explanation: Both Romeo ('brawling love, loving hate') and Juliet ('happy dagger') use oxymorons in their most emotionally extreme moments. They also appear in other characters' speeches — Friar Laurence in particular ('virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied'). Spotting and analysing oxymorons is one of the most rewarding ways to write about the play's language.

questions.q: How does the language Romeo uses about love change between the start of the play (when he's in love with Rosaline) and after he meets Juliet?
questions.a: When pining for Rosaline, Romeo's language is heavily clichéd and full of conventional oxymorons ('O brawling love, O loving hate') — Shakespeare is mocking the courtly-love tradition. After meeting Juliet, his language becomes more original and sincere — the sun metaphor ('Juliet is the sun'), the genuine wonder of the balcony scene. His shift in language signals a genuine emotional transformation: he moves from being in love with the idea of love to being in love with a real person.
questions.hint: Compare 'brawling love, loving hate' with 'Juliet is the sun'.
questions.explanation: This is a classic exam question. The key insight is that Shakespeare's changing language is a deliberate signal of Romeo's growth — he begins as a stock Petrarchan lover (whose love is performative and exaggerated) and becomes a sincere lover whose imagery is fresh and personal. This shift is what makes the early-Rosaline scenes important rather than just throwaway.

questions.q: What does the prologue mean by 'a pair of star-crossed lovers take their life'?
questions.a: 'Star-crossed' means having destinies that are opposed or thwarted by the stars — that is, by fate. In Elizabethan belief, the stars determined human destiny, and a 'crossing' of stars meant a fated misfortune. The line tells the audience at the very start of the play that Romeo and Juliet are doomed: they will die. This creates dramatic irony — the audience knows the ending, while the characters don't.
questions.hint: Stars in Elizabethan culture symbolised fate.
questions.explanation: Telling the audience the ending up front is unusual and important. It changes the play from a 'will they survive?' story to a 'how does this unfold?' tragedy. It also reinforces the play's theme of fate vs free will: the lovers seem to choose their actions, but each choice leads them closer to the ending the stars decreed.

questions.q: How are Romeo and Juliet's deaths portrayed differently in the language Shakespeare gives them?
questions.a: Romeo's death is framed as a final act of love ('Here's to my love!') and dignified surrender — he drinks poison and his last word is 'die', after a kiss. Juliet's death is more violent — she stabs herself with Romeo's dagger — and the language is shockingly intimate ('O happy dagger! This is thy sheath'). Both characters welcome death as reunion, but Juliet's act is more physically forceful and more openly tied to the body and to sexual imagery. Romeo dies passively (poison); Juliet dies actively (a blade).
questions.hint: Romeo drinks; Juliet stabs. How does the language reflect this?
questions.explanation: This is a useful comparative point in essays. Some critics argue Juliet is portrayed as the more decisive and assertive of the lovers — even in death. Others argue both are equally devoted but use different metaphors. A strong essay would acknowledge the complexity and use specific quotes to support whichever view is being argued.

questions.q: 'Romeo and Juliet is more about fate than free will.' To what extent do you agree?
questions.a: Strong evidence for fate: the prologue describes the lovers as 'star-crossed', signalling predetermined doom; Romeo refers to 'fortune' as if it controls his actions; both characters have moments of premonition (Romeo: 'I fear too early, for my mind misgives / Some consequence yet hanging in the stars'); the timing of events (the letter not reaching Romeo, Juliet waking moments too late) is almost supernaturally precise. Strong evidence against (in favour of free will): every disaster is caused by a specific human choice — Romeo's impulsive marriage, his decision to kill Tybalt, his refusal to wait when he hears of Juliet's 'death'; the parents' feud, Friar Laurence's risky plan, Tybalt's aggression. Conclusion: the play deliberately maintains the tension between the two. The characters seem to choose freely, but their choices fulfil the fate the prologue announced. Shakespeare suggests that human passion and social structures can produce outcomes that feel like fate even when no supernatural force is involved.
questions.hint: There's evidence for both sides. A strong answer engages with that tension.
questions.explanation: 'To what extent' questions reward a balanced argument with a clear judgement. The smartest essays note that fate and free will work together in the play — Shakespeare uses fate as a frame, but the characters' impulsive, passionate choices are what actually drive the tragedy. Quote both the prologue and specific human decisions to demonstrate the tension.
